one_line_takeaway: A SoK that systematizes agentic-AI security into 7 design dimensions, 6 attack vectors under 3 threat models, 7 CIA-triad-spanning risk categories, and a defense-in-depth taxonomy, then uses a 6-agent case study to show that even widely used coding and web agents (Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenHands, Browser-use, Nanobrowser, Skyvern) implement only partial coverage of the defense classes the framework identifies as necessary.
key_finding: The review corpus yields 128 papers, comprising 51 attack methods and 60 defense methods (the remainder cover both or are case studies without new methods). The design-dimension-to-risk mapping shows that Input Trust, Memory, and Tool dimensions primarily drive attack-surface risk (R1); Workflow flexibility primarily drives model risks (R2-R4, e.g., control-flow hijacking, hallucination-triggered real-world actions); and Access Sensitivity, Action, and User Interface dimensions determine the severity of consequence risks (R5-R7). Risks cascade: R1 (expanded attack surface) feeds R2-R4 (model risks), which in turn amplify R5-R7 (consequence risks) -- illustrated with the EchoLeak incident as a case of R1 -> R3 -> R5 cascading without user interaction. In the real-world agent case study (Table 3), the authors find coding agents (Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenHands) prioritize output guardrails, access control, and human-in-the-loop validation for filesystem/shell actions over input sanitization, while all three provide only partial monitoring (raw logs without anomaly detection). Web agents (Browser-use, Nanobrowser, Skyvern) commonly deploy input guardrails, output guardrails, credential management, and monitoring given their much larger untrusted-input surface, but 'most defenses provide only partial protection, with manually curated controls and incomplete coverage,' and none of the surveyed systems pair browsing telemetry with automated anomaly detection. The authors also note that layering defenses can produce 'emergent misalignment' where one mechanism weakens another (e.g., a sanitizer stripping safety instructions a downstream guardrail relies on).
core_contribution: A systematization-of-knowledge (SoK) survey of AI agent security, positioned by the authors as 'the first comprehensive survey of AI agent security' and 'the first systematic framework for understanding the security risks and defense strategies of AI agents.' It contributes: (1) a 7-dimension agent design-space taxonomy (input trust, access sensitivity, workflow, action, memory, tool, user interface), each mapped along a flexibility spectrum and connected to MITRE ATLAS and the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications; (2) an attack taxonomy of 6 attack vectors (V1 indirect prompt injection, V2 malicious data injection, V3 tool poisoning/manipulation, V4 direct prompt injection, V5 model poisoning, V6 memory poisoning) organized under 3 threat models (external, user-level, internal adversary), plus a 7-category risk taxonomy (R1 heterogeneous untrusted interfaces, R2 wrong instruction following, R3 unconstrained/unsafe data flow, R4 hallucination and model mistakes, R5 private data leakage, R6 unintended/unauthorized action and data corruption, R7 resource drain/DoS) spanning the CIA triad, with an analysis of how design dimensions map to risks and how risks cascade/amplify one another; (3) a systematized defense landscape (Table 2) organized into Runtime Protection (input guardrails, output guardrails, information flow control/taint tracking, monitoring, human-in-the-loop), Secure-by-Design (privilege separation, formal verification), Identity and Access Management (identity management, access control, credential management), and Component Hardening (model hardening, tool hardening), each mapped to the risks it covers, plus a proposed new security goal, 'Contextual Security', extending Nissenbaum's contextual integrity to agent action alignment; (4) a case study of 6 real-world open-source agents (Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenHands as coding agents; Browser-use, Nanobrowser, Skyvern as web agents) scored (full/partial/none) against 10 defense classes (Table 3) to expose concrete coverage gaps in deployed systems.
threat_model: The paper's own taxonomy defines three threat models by attacker access at attack-execution time: (1) External adversary -- cannot directly interact with the agent but can manipulate external resources the agent retrieves/processes (enables V1 indirect prompt injection, V2 malicious data injection, V3 tool poisoning); this is described as 'the most constrained yet highly realistic scenario.' (2) User-level adversary -- has access to agent inputs and can directly feed or embed malicious content in otherwise benign user inputs (enables V4 direct prompt injection). (3) Internal adversary -- can access some or all components inside the agent (enables V5 model poisoning via backdoor injection, V6 memory poisoning); characterized as the 'strongest assumption... most severe threat... but less practical.' The paper explicitly scopes out model-internal attacks such as model inversion, reasoning they 'do not fundamentally worsen in agentic contexts' since agents interact only through inference-time input-output interfaces.
limitations: Limitations are the paper's own stated scope boundaries and identified open challenges, not external critique: (1) the survey explicitly excludes attacks on model internals (e.g., model inversion) since agents interact only via inference-time I/O; (2) side-channel attacks (timing, API call sequences, network traffic patterns) are flagged as an unstudied area the authors 'defer... to future work' rather than analyze; (3) the real-world case study covers only 6 open-source agents at specific pinned versions and is explicitly a time snapshot -- the authors note 'these agents are actively evolving'; (4) the case study focuses on system-level defenses rather than component hardening; (5) per-category open-challenges sections repeatedly flag unresolved problems the surveyed literature has not solved: input/output guardrails suffer false positive/negative tradeoffs and are bypassed by adaptive attacks; IFC/taint-tracking methods incur substantial runtime overhead and label creep; monitoring struggles to distinguish stochastic benign vs. malicious agent behavior and faces unresolved storage/privacy costs for long-running logs; privilege separation causes utility loss and lacks strategies for complex real-world environments (web, filesystems, databases); formal verification methods are built for deterministic code and struggle to capture probabilistic LLM behavior; identity management lacks standardized/interoperable frameworks across platforms; credential management in agent stacks still relies on ad-hoc practices (e.g., unencrypted environment variables).

quantitative_results: Literature review corpus: 128 papers reviewed, covering 2023 to October 2025
Of the 128 papers: 51 introduce attack methods, 60 introduce defense methods, and the remainder address both or are case studies without new methods
Design-space taxonomy: 7 agent design dimensions (input trust, access sensitivity, workflow, action, memory, tool, user interface)
Attack taxonomy: 6 attack vectors (V1-V6) organized under 3 threat models (external, user-level, internal adversary)
Risk taxonomy: 7 security risk categories (R1-R7) spanning confidentiality, integrity, and availability

Real-world agent case study: 6 open-source agents evaluated (Codex v0.53.0, Gemini CLI v0.13.0, OpenHands v0.59.0, Browser Use v0.9.0, Nanobrowser v0.1.12, Skyvern v0.2.20) against 10 defense mechanism classes (input guardrail, output guardrail, access control, IFC, monitoring, HITL, privilege separation, formal verification, identity management, credential management)
